name: Shoku Nihongi
shortDescription: The Shoku Nihongi is the second of Japan's Six National Histories, chronicling events from 697 to 791 CE.
description: The Shoku Nihongi (続日本紀, literally 'Chronicle of Japan Continued') is the second of the Six National Histories of Japan, commissioned by Emperor Kanmu and completed in 797 CE. It covers the period from 697 CE, following the abdication of Empress Jitō, to 791 CE, spanning the reigns of Emperors Monmu, Gemmei, Genshō, Shōmu, Kōken/Shōtoku, Junnin, Kōnin, and briefly, Kanmu. Composed in classical Chinese, like its predecessor the Nihon Shoki, the Shoku Nihongi provides a detailed account of political events, courtly life, religious developments (particularly the rise of Buddhism), and social customs of the Nara period. It is an invaluable resource for understanding the development of Japanese society, governance, and cultural identity during this formative era. The text includes imperial edicts, biographical details of prominent figures, and records of significant events, offering insights into the complex interplay of power, religion, and tradition that shaped early Japanese history. Its meticulous record-keeping provides historians and scholars with a rich tapestry of information about the lives and times of the people who lived during this period.
subtitle: A chronicle of Nara Japan's rise.
author: Fujiwara no Tsugunari et al.
significance: Offers invaluable insights into the Nara period of Japanese history and the development of Japanese identity.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: While not strictly a mythological text, the Shoku Nihongi builds upon the mythological framework established in the Nihon Shoki. It continues the narrative of the imperial lineage, tracing its divine origins back to Amaterasu, the sun goddess. The text implicitly reinforces the legitimacy of the imperial line through its historical accounts, framing events within a narrative of divine mandate and cosmic order. While it focuses on historical events, it also contains accounts of omens, portents, and supernatural occurrences that reflect the prevailing beliefs of the time. The establishment of Buddhist temples and the patronage of Buddhist monks are frequently mentioned, indicating the growing influence of Buddhist cosmology and beliefs within Japanese society. The text also alludes to earlier myths when discussing the origins of certain clans or the significance of specific locations.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: The Shoku Nihongi served as an official record of the Japanese court and its activities during the Nara period. Its primary role was to document the reigns of the emperors, providing a detailed account of their policies, decrees, and interactions with other members of the court. The text also served as a historical precedent, informing future generations about the proper conduct of rulers and the importance of maintaining social order. By meticulously recording events, the Shoku Nihongi aimed to legitimize the imperial line and solidify its authority. It also played a significant role in shaping Japanese cultural identity by preserving and transmitting traditions, customs, and values from one generation to the next. The chronicle is significant for its detailed accounts of the spread of Buddhism, including the construction of Todai-ji temple and the Great Buddha statue.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: The Shoku Nihongi, being a historical text, does not lend itself to symbolic interpretation in the same way as explicitly mythological narratives. However, the text can be interpreted as a symbolic representation of imperial power and the continuity of Japanese history. The meticulous record-keeping and the emphasis on order and stability can be seen as symbolic of the desire to create a harmonious and prosperous society under the rule of the emperor. The inclusion of Buddhist elements also symbolizes the growing integration of Buddhist beliefs and practices into Japanese culture. The text's focus on the lives of the elite and the activities of the court can be interpreted as a symbolic representation of the social hierarchy and the importance of maintaining proper relationships within the ruling class. The chronicle's emphasis on ritual and ceremony underscores the symbolic importance of these practices in maintaining social cohesion and reinforcing imperial authority.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: The Shoku Nihongi remains an invaluable resource for historians and scholars studying the Nara period of Japanese history. It provides a detailed and comprehensive account of events, policies, and cultural developments that shaped early Japanese society. The text has been used extensively by historians to reconstruct the political, social, and religious landscape of the Nara period. It has also influenced subsequent historical writings and cultural productions. The Shoku Nihongi continues to be studied in universities and research institutions around the world, and its insights are essential for understanding the development of Japanese identity and the evolution of Japanese culture. Its influence extends beyond academia, as it informs popular understandings of Japanese history and continues to shape cultural narratives about the nation's past. Its meticulous record-keeping has set a precedent for subsequent historical chronicles and continues to inspire historical research.

sources.author: W.G. Aston
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, which provides essential context for understanding the Shoku Nihongi.

sources.author: J. Edward Kidder
sources.description: Provides historical context for the rise of Buddhism during the Nara period, as documented in the Shoku Nihongi.

associations.name: Imperial Authority
associations.significance: The text reinforces the legitimacy and power of the Imperial line.

associations.name: Buddhism
associations.significance: Records the growing influence and integration of Buddhist beliefs and practices.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Records of the Grand Historian (Shiji)
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Comprehensive historical account that establishes a narrative of dynastic rule and cultural identity.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Anglo-Saxon Chronicle
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: A historical record tracking the reigns of rulers and important events, shaping national identity.

companions.name: Nihon Shoki
companions.description: The first of the Six National Histories, providing the mythological and historical foundation for the Shoku Nihongi.

companions.name: Emperor Shomu
companions.description: A central figure whose reign is extensively documented in the Shoku Nihongi, known for his patronage of Buddhism.

worshipPractices.name: Imperial Rituals
worshipPractices.description: While not worship in the traditional sense, the Shoku Nihongi documents the performance of imperial rituals that reinforced the emperor's authority and maintained social order.

worshipPractices.name: Buddhist Ceremonies
worshipPractices.description: The chronicle describes various Buddhist ceremonies and festivals that were observed during the Nara period, reflecting the growing influence of Buddhism on Japanese society.

festivals.name: Buddhist Festivals
festivals.description: The Shoku Nihongi records the observance of various Buddhist festivals, such as Vesak, which celebrated the birth of the Buddha.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Shoku Nihongi continues to be a vital resource for understanding Japanese history and culture. Its insights inform historical research, cultural productions, and popular understandings of the nation's past.
